english: Freie Universität Berlin, Dahlem. October. Verumius enters the world of the German university. Learn the key campus words and discover what makes German university culture unique.
Learn 20 words about university life
Discover the academic register of German
Follow Verumius on his first day on campus

Freie Universität Berlin, Dahlem. An October morning. The 1960s buildings stand between old plane trees. Students cycle past with coffee cups. Bulletin boards overflow with flyers. Verumius holds a campus map and turns it upside down.
Excuse me, where is lecture hall 1A? I have my first lecture in a moment.
A student points left. Verumius walks right. Another student redirects him. German university campuses have no mercy for newcomers.
If only I had studied the campus map beforehand. Now I am the new guy again.
the university
Feminine noun. Plural: die Universitäten.\nOften shortened to 'Uni' in spoken German.

A: At which university do you study?
B: At the Freie Universität Berlin.
A: Do you like the university?
B: Yes, the university has a great campus.

From Latin 'universitas' (community of scholars). Germany has over 400 Hochschulen. Freie Universität Berlin was founded in 1948 in West Berlin as a 'free' alternative to the Soviet-controlled Humboldt. The name itself is political history.
the lecture hall
Masculine noun. Hör (listen) + Saal (hall).\nPlural: die Hörsäle.

A: Where is lecture hall 1A?
B: On the ground floor, left around the corner.
A: How big is the lecture hall?
B: It has 500 seats.
'Hör' (hear/listen) + 'Saal' (hall). A 'listening hall.' German university Hörsäle can seat 500+ students. The word tells you your job: sit and listen.
the university cafeteria
Feminine noun. Plural: die Mensen.\nSubsidized student cafeteria.

A: Shall we go to the cafeteria?
B: Sure! What is there today?
A: Schnitzel with potato salad.
B: The cafeteria is the best place on campus.
From Latin 'mensa' (table). German Mensa meals cost 2-4 EUR for students. A democratic space: everyone eats the same food at the same price. Subsidized by the state.
the seminar
Neuter noun. A small discussion class.\nContrast with Vorlesung (large lecture).

A: Do you have a seminar today?
B: Yes, linguistics seminar at 2 PM.
A: How many people are in the seminar?
B: Only 25. You have to actively participate.

From Latin 'seminarium' (seed plot). A Seminar has 20-30 students and expects active participation. A Vorlesung has 200+ and expects listening. The difference is fundamental to German university culture.
the lecture
Feminine noun. Vor (before) + Lesung (reading).\n'Eine Vorlesung besuchen' (to attend a lecture).

A: How was the lecture?
B: Interesting, but very fast.
A: Could you write everything down?
B: No, the lecture was too dense.
'Vor' (before) + 'Lesung' (reading). A 'reading aloud.' Professors traditionally 'read' their material aloud. German students show appreciation by knocking on the desk, not clapping.
the degree program
Masculine noun. Studien (studies) + Gang (path).\n'Welchen Studiengang belegst du?'

A: Which degree program did you choose?
B: Linguistics as a major.
A: Is the degree program difficult?
B: Yes, but very exciting.
'Studien' (studies) + 'Gang' (path/course). Your path through academic study. German universities offer over 20,000 different Studiengänge. The word captures it: a journey through knowledge.
the lecturer (male)
Masculine noun. N-Deklination: den Dozenten.\nBelow Professor, above Tutor.

A: Who gives the lecture?
B: The lecturer, Mr. Meier.
A: Is he strict?
B: No, the lecturer explains everything very clearly.
From Latin 'docens' (teaching). Below Professor but above Tutor in the academic hierarchy. Dozenten do the heavy lifting of university teaching.
the lecturer (female)
Feminine noun. Dozent + -in.\nGender-neutral alternative: Dozierende.

A: Do you know the lecturer?
B: Yes, Dr. Schmidt. She is very competent.
A: Is the lecturer nice?
B: Yes, but demanding.
Dozent + '-in.' The feminine form follows the standard pattern. German universities now use 'Dozierende' as a gender-neutral alternative, but Dozent/Dozentin remains standard in daily use.
the faculty, the department
Feminine noun. Plural: die Fakultäten.\n'Die Philosophische Fakultät.'

A: Which faculty do you belong to?
B: To the Faculty of Arts.
A: How many faculties does the university have?
B: Twelve. Each faculty is like a small university.
From Latin 'facultas' (ability). German universities are organized into Fakultäten: Philosophische, Naturwissenschaftliche, Juristische. Each is like a small university within the university.
the enrollment, the registration
Feminine noun. Ein (in) + Schreibung (writing).\n'Die Einschreibung abschließen.'

A: Have you done the enrollment yet?
B: Yes, since yesterday I am enrolled.
A: When was the deadline for enrollment?
B: Until October 15th.
You 'write yourself in' to the university. The Einschreibung deadline is sacred. Miss it and you wait a semester. A classic example of Nominalisierung: einschreiben becomes Einschreibung.
the formal enrollment
Feminine noun. Formal synonym of Einschreibung.\nMore bureaucratic register.

A: What is the difference between Einschreibung and Immatrikulation?
B: There is none. Immatrikulation just sounds more formal.
A: Do you have your enrollment certificate?
B: Yes, I need it for everything.

From Latin 'immatriculare' (to enter in a register). Your Immatrikulationsbescheinigung (enrollment certificate) is your student ID for everything. One of the longest German compound words students encounter.
to study (at university)
Regular verb. NOT the same as 'lernen.'\n'Ich studiere Linguistik' = enrolled in a degree.

A: What do you study?
B: I study linguistics.
A: Since when have you been studying?
B: Since this semester. I have only been studying for a short time.

From Latin 'studere' (to be eager). Not the same as 'lernen.' 'Ich studiere Linguistik' means you are enrolled in a degree. 'Ich lerne Deutsch' means you are learning the language. Two different words for two different activities.
to formally enroll
Regular, reflexive: sich immatrikulieren.\nThe bureaucratic verb for becoming a student.

A: Have you enrolled yet?
B: Yes, I enrolled last week.
A: How was the process?
B: A lot of paperwork. Typically German.
See Immatrikulation. Reflexive: 'sich immatrikulieren.' You immatrikulieren yourself. Once done, you get your Studentenausweis. The bureaucratic verb for becoming an official student.
academic
Adjective. 'Akademisches Viertel' = lectures\nstart 15 minutes late (written 'c.t.').

A: What does 'academic quarter' mean?
B: The lecture starts 15 minutes later.
A: That is an academic tradition?
B: Yes, for centuries!

From Greek 'akademeia' (Plato's school). 'Akademisches Viertel' means lectures start 15 minutes late. Written 'c.t.' (cum tempore). If a lecture is at 10:00 c.t., it starts at 10:15. This is tradition, not laziness.
scientific, scholarly
Adjective. Wissenschaft (scholarship) + -lich.\nCovers ALL scholarship, not just natural science.

A: Is your work scholarly?
B: Yes, it follows scholarly methods.
A: What does academic writing mean?
B: Sources, structure, objective analysis.
'Wissenschaft' (science/scholarship) + '-lich.' 'Wissenschaftliches Arbeiten' is a course every German student must take. The word covers ALL scholarship, not just natural science.
fundamental, foundational
Adjective. Grund (ground) + legend (laying).\n'Grundlegende Kenntnisse' (fundamental knowledge).

A: Are the first lectures fundamental?
B: Yes, they lay the foundations.
A: What is fundamental for linguistics?
B: Phonetics, syntax, and semantics are fundamental.

'Grund' (ground/foundation) + 'legend' (laying). The word paints a picture: laying the foundation. 'Grundlegende Kenntnisse' is used constantly in academic writing. You cannot skip the grundlegenden concepts.
in particular, especially
Adverb. In + s + besondere.\nFormal register. 'Insbesondere in der Linguistik.'

A: What interests you about university?
B: Linguistics, in particular phonetics.
A: Why phonetics in particular?
B: Because sounds fascinate me.
'In' + 's' + 'besondere.' A formal adverb beloved by German academics. One word where English needs two. Elegant and precise. You will hear it in every lecture and read it in every paper.
in principle, fundamentally
Adverb. Grundsatz (principle) + -lich.\n'Grundsätzlich gilt...' (In principle, ... applies).

A: Can you change your degree program?
B: In principle yes, but there are deadlines.
A: So fundamentally possible?
B: Yes, fundamentally speaking.
'Grundsatz' (principle) + '-lich.' A hedging word: it says 'in theory' while leaving room for exceptions. 'Grundsätzlich gilt...' opens many an academic argument.
by means of, on the basis of
Preposition + Genitiv. An (at) + Hand (hand).\n'Anhand der Daten' (on the basis of the data).

A: How do you prove a thesis?
B: By means of data and sources.
A: By means of which method?
B: By means of empirical research.
'An' (at) + 'Hand' (hand). Literally 'at the hand of.' Academic German uses this constantly. Always followed by Genitiv. You will see 'anhand' on every page of every German research paper.
with regard to, concerning
Preposition + Genitiv. Hin (toward) + Sicht (view).\nFormal academic register.

A: What does the professor say with regard to the exam?
B: With regard to the exam there are new rules.
A: Regarding the date?
B: The date remains the same according to the plan.
'Hin' (toward) + 'Sicht' (view) + '-lich.' A formal preposition with Genitiv. One of the hallmarks of academic register. You will almost never hear it in casual conversation.
The word is a compound: Hör (hören/zuhören) + Saal (hall). What do students do in a lecture?
The formal process of registering at a university. You 'schreiben sich ein' (enroll).
For his studies Verumius must complete the enrollment before October 15th.
Verumius finds the lecture hall. Ten minutes late, but nobody looks up. Academic quarter. He sits in the last row and exhales. The university has not spit him out yet. Day one, item one: survived.
The language of research. Learn the key words for academic work. Recognize the pattern between verbs and their nominalized forms.
Learn 20 words from the language of research
Recognize the pattern of Nominalisierung (verb to noun)
Build your academic German toolkit
the research
Feminine noun. From 'forschen' (to research).\n'Forschung und Lehre' (research and teaching).

A: What is the goal of your research?
B: I investigate language patterns.
A: Is research hard?
B: Yes, but the research is fun.
From 'forschen' (to research). 'Forschung und Lehre' (research and teaching) is the dual mission of every German university. The Forschung half is what makes a university different from a school.
the hypothesis
Feminine noun. Plural: die Hypothesen.\n'Eine Hypothese aufstellen' (to put forward a hypothesis).

A: Do you have a hypothesis yet?
B: Yes, my hypothesis is that nominalization makes texts harder.
A: Interesting hypothesis.
B: I still need to test it.

From Greek 'hypothesis' (foundation). German academic writing demands that you state your Hypothese clearly before investigating it. 'Eine Hypothese aufstellen' is the first step of every research project.
the method
Feminine noun. Plural: die Methoden.\n'Wissenschaftliche Methoden' (scientific methods).

A: Which method are you using?
B: A quantitative method with statistics.
A: Is the method reliable?
B: Yes, the method has been tested many times.
From Greek 'methodos' (pursuit of knowledge). 'Wissenschaftliche Methoden' is the backbone of German research. Every Hausarbeit must describe its Methode explicitly. No method, no scholarship.
the statistics
Feminine noun. 'Laut Statistik' (according to statistics).\nOriginally meant data about the state.

A: Do you need statistics for your studies?
B: Yes, statistics is mandatory.
A: Do you like statistics?
B: According to statistics, 50% of students do not like it.
From Latin 'statisticum' (of the state). Originally meant data about the state. Now covers all quantitative analysis. 'Laut Statistik' (according to statistics) starts many German arguments.
the study (research)
Feminine noun. Plural: die Studien.\nNot the same as 'Studium' (university studies).

A: Do you know the new study?
B: Which study do you mean?
A: The study about language learning.
B: Yes, a very interesting study.
From Latin 'studium.' A specific research project, not your university studies. 'Eine Studie durchführen' (to conduct a study). A Studie produces Ergebnisse (results).
the analysis
Feminine noun. Plural: die Analysen.\n'Eine gründliche Analyse' (a thorough analysis).

A: How is the analysis going?
B: The analysis of the data is almost done.
A: Does the analysis show something new?
B: Yes, the analysis confirms our hypothesis.
From Greek 'analysis' (breaking up). Academic German loves this word. The verb form 'analysieren' is equally common. Analyse (noun) and analysieren (verb) are a Nominalisierung pair.
the result, the outcome
Neuter noun. Plural: die Ergebnisse.\n'Die Ergebnisse zeigen...' (The results show...).

A: What do the results show?
B: The results are surprising.
A: Positively surprising?
B: Yes, the result exceeds our expectations.
From 'ergeben' (to result in). 'Die Ergebnisse zeigen...' opens paragraphs in every German research paper. Neuter because of the Er- prefix pattern. Plural: Ergebnisse.
the investigation, the examination
Feminine noun. From 'untersuchen' (to investigate).\nClassic Nominalisierung: verb + -ung.

A: What is the topic of your investigation?
B: The investigation deals with language patterns.
A: How long does the investigation take?
B: The investigation runs for another three months.

From 'untersuchen' (to investigate). Notice the pattern: the verb 'untersuchen' becomes the noun 'Untersuchung' with the -ung suffix. This is Nominalisierung. Academic German loves this transformation.
the source
Feminine noun. Plural: die Quellen.\nLiterally 'spring' (as in water).

A: How many sources do you have?
B: Over twenty sources.
A: Are the sources reliable?
B: Yes, only academic sources.
Literally 'spring/source' (as in water). Knowledge flows from its source. 'Quellenangabe' (source citation) is mandatory in German academia. Forgetting your Quellen is academic suicide.
the thesis, the proposition
Feminine noun. Plural: die Thesen.\n'Eine These verteidigen' (to defend a thesis).

A: What is your thesis?
B: My thesis: nominalization makes texts more formal.
A: Can you support the thesis?
B: Yes, on the basis of the data.
From Greek 'thesis' (placement). Martin Luther nailed 95 Thesen to a church door. The word carries weight in German. 'Eine These verteidigen' is what every student must do.
to analyze
Regular verb. From Analyse.\n'Daten analysieren' (to analyze data).

A: Have you analyzed the data yet?
B: Yes, I am currently analyzing the last results.
A: What did you analyze?
B: Text patterns in academic papers.
From Analyse. Notice: Analyse (noun) and analysieren (verb) are a pair. The noun came first from Greek; the verb was formed from it. This reverse process is called Verbalisierung.
to investigate, to examine
Inseparable verb. Unter (under) + suchen (search).\n'Wir untersuchen den Zusammenhang.'

A: What are you investigating?
B: I am investigating the connection between language and thought.
A: How are you investigating that?
B: With interviews and text analyses.
'Unter' (under) + 'suchen' (to search). Searching underneath the surface. The noun: Untersuchung (investigation). A classic Nominalisierung pair: untersuchen becomes die Untersuchung.
to define
Regular verb. From Latin 'definire.'\n'Den Begriff definieren' (to define the term).

A: How do you define 'nominalization'?
B: I define it as the transformation of verbs into nouns.
A: Well defined!
B: You must define terms clearly.
From Latin 'definire' (to set boundaries). Every German Hausarbeit starts by defining its key terms. Academic writing rule number one: define before you argue.
to formulate, to phrase
Regular verb. From Latin 'formulare.'\n'Eine Hypothese formulieren.'

A: How would you phrase that?
B: I would phrase it like this: The data show...
A: Well phrased.
B: You must formulate precisely.
From Latin 'formulare.' 'Eine Hypothese formulieren' (to formulate a hypothesis). 'Wie würden Sie das formulieren?' is what advisors ask students constantly. Precision in phrasing is everything.
to interpret
Regular verb. From Latin 'interpretari.'\n'Ergebnisse interpretieren' (to interpret results).

A: How do you interpret the results?
B: I interpret them positively.
A: Can one interpret them differently?
B: Yes, one must interpret various possibilities.

From Latin 'interpretari.' Data alone says nothing. The Interpretation is where academic skill shows. 'Wie interpretieren Sie diese Daten?' is the question that separates good researchers from great ones.
to compare
Strong verb: verglich, verglichen. Inseparable.\n'Zwei Studien vergleichen.'

A: Have you compared the two studies?
B: Yes, I compared them carefully.
A: And the comparison?
B: The results are similar.
'Ver-' + 'gleichen' (to equal/level). You make things 'equal' to see the differences. Strong verb: vergleichen/verglich/verglichen. Der Vergleich is the nominalized form.
empirical, based on observation
Adjective. The opposite of 'theoretisch.'\n'Empirische Forschung' (empirical research).

A: Is your research empirical?
B: Yes, purely empirical. Everything is based on data.
A: Not theoretical?
B: No, empirical. Observation before theory.
From Greek 'empeiria' (experience). Research based on data, not just theory. The opposite of 'theoretisch.' A key academic distinction in every German research seminar.
theoretical
Adjective. From Greek 'theoria' (contemplation).\n'Theoretisch möglich' (theoretically possible).

A: Is that theoretically possible?
B: Theoretically yes, practically difficult.
A: Do you need a theoretical framework?
B: Yes, every paper needs a theoretical framework.
From Greek 'theoria' (contemplation). 'Theoretisch möglich' always implies 'but practically unlikely.' Academic work needs both: theoretischer Rahmen (theoretical framework) + empirische Daten.
systematic
Adjective. From Greek 'systema.'\n'Systematische Untersuchung.'

A: Are you proceeding systematically?
B: Yes, always systematically. Step by step.
A: Why is systematic important?
B: Without a systematic approach, no scholarship.
From Greek 'systema.' German scholarship values system above all. A systematischer Ansatz (systematic approach) is not optional; it is expected. Unsystematisch is an insult in academia.
objective
Adjective. The opposite of 'subjektiv.'\n'Bleiben Sie objektiv' (Stay objective).

A: Is your analysis objective?
B: I try to stay objective.
A: That is hard.
B: Yes, but being objective is the goal.
From Latin 'objectivus.' The academic ideal: your personal opinion stays out. 'Bleiben Sie objektiv' is the professor's mantra. 'Objektive Analyse' is what every paper must deliver.

German constantly turns verbs and adjectives into nouns. The pattern:

Verb + -ung = feminine noun:
untersuchen > die Untersuchung
entwickeln > die Entwicklung
forschen > die Forschung

This is Nominalisierung. Academic German LOVES it.

A single noun can replace an entire clause:
'Die Untersuchung der Daten' = 'dass man die Daten untersucht.'

Nominalisierung makes academic writing denser and more formal.

Nominalisierung is the tool of academic compression. Instead of 'Wir haben untersucht, wie...' you write 'Die Untersuchung zeigt...' The sentence becomes shorter, more impersonal, and more formal. That is the academic style. Spoken German prefers the verbal style. Written German prefers the nominal style. Both are correct, but context determines the choice.
Look for the -ung ending. Which verb does it come from? Unter + suchen.
to do research, to investigate
Regular verb. From 'Forschung' (research).\nThe verb behind every scientific inquiry.

A: Are you currently researching a topic?
B: Yes, I research language change.
A: How long does one research for a term paper?
B: At least two to three weeks.

'Forschung' (research) is the nominalized form. Germany invests heavily in Forschung und Entwicklung (research and development). 'Der Forscher' and 'die Forscherin' are the agent nouns. Renowned for Grundlagenforschung (basic research).
An informed guess about what research will reveal. Of Greek origin.
Before doing research, you must put forward a hypothesis.
Academic German uses nominalized forms, precise adjectives, and avoids personal opinion markers.
Survive your first German lecture. Learn vocabulary for argumentation and logical thinking. Master the Konjunktiv II past for hypothetical past events.
Learn 20 words for argumentation and conclusions
Master the Konjunktiv II past (hätte/wäre + Partizip II)
Follow an academic lecture in German
Lecture hall 1A. Two hundred students. The professor speaks freely, without notes, pacing the stage. Slides dense with text. Verumius writes as fast as he can. Every third word is new.
The professor says: 'Had they recognized this connection earlier, the development would have gone differently.' Verumius pauses. Past tense, but hypothetical. A new grammar.
If only I had read the text beforehand. Then I would have understood more. But this is how I learn: under pressure.
the connection, the context
Masculine noun. Zusammen (together) + Hang (slope).\n'In diesem Zusammenhang' (in this context).

A: In what context does this stand?
B: In the context of the research question.
A: Do you see the connection?
B: Yes, the connection is becoming clear now.
'Zusammen' (together) + 'Hang' (hang/slope). Things that 'hang together.' 'In diesem Zusammenhang' opens paragraphs in every academic paper. Plural: Zusammenhänge.
the meaning, the significance
Feminine noun. From 'bedeuten' (to mean).\n'Von großer Bedeutung' (of great significance).

A: What is the meaning of this term?
B: The meaning is 'connection.'
A: Does it have a deeper significance?
B: Yes, the significance goes beyond the dictionary.
From 'bedeuten' (to mean). Academic German distinguishes between Bedeutung (meaning) and Wichtigkeit (importance). Bedeutung is deeper. 'Von großer Bedeutung' signals that something truly matters.
the development
Feminine noun. Ent- (un-) + Wicklung (winding).\nLiterally 'unwinding,' something that unfolds.

A: How was the development last year?
B: The development was very positive.
A: What development do you expect?
B: A steady upward development.
'Ent-' (un-) + 'Wicklung' (winding). Literally 'unwinding.' Something that unfolds over time. 'Die historische Entwicklung' frames every academic narrative. Nominalisierung from 'entwickeln.'
the presentation, the depiction
Feminine noun. Dar (there) + Stellung (placement).\n'Die Darstellung der Ergebnisse.'

A: How is the presentation of the results?
B: The presentation is clear and well-organized.
A: A good presentation is important.
B: Yes, the presentation determines the success.
'Dar' (there) + 'Stellung' (placement). You 'place something there' for others to see. Academic writing IS Darstellung: presenting your findings for the world.
the conclusion, the inference
Feminine noun. Schluss + Folgerung.\nTriple compound: Schluss + Folge + -ung.

A: What is your conclusion?
B: My conclusion: nominalization is productive.
A: A logical conclusion.
B: Yes, the conclusion is based on data.
'Schluss' (conclusion) + 'Folgerung' (inference). Triple compound. 'Welche Schlussfolgerung ziehen Sie?' (What conclusion do you draw?). The crown of academic argument.
the foundation, the basis
Feminine noun. Grund (ground) + Lage (position).\n'Auf der Grundlage von Daten.'

A: What is the foundation of your work?
B: Empirical data form the foundation.
A: Is the foundation stable?
B: Yes, a solid foundation.
'Grund' (ground) + 'Lage' (position/layer). The bedrock on which your argument stands. 'Auf der Grundlage von Daten' appears in every serious German research paper.
the state of affairs, the facts
Masculine noun. Sach (matter) + Verhalt (condition).\n'Den Sachverhalt darstellen.'

A: Can you explain the state of affairs?
B: The state of affairs is complex.
A: Present the facts simply.
B: The facts: language constantly changes.
'Sach' (thing/matter) + 'Verhalt' (condition). Pure academic German. No emotional coloring, just the facts. 'Den Sachverhalt darstellen' means to present the facts objectively.
the change, the transformation
Feminine noun. Ver- + Änderung (change).\nStronger than 'Änderung' alone.

A: Do you see a change?
B: Yes, a fundamental change.
A: Since when has this change existed?
B: The change began ten years ago.
'Ver-' + 'Änderung' (change). The ver- prefix implies thoroughness. 'Grundlegende Veränderungen' (fundamental changes). More complete than a simple Änderung.
to justify, to give reasons
Inseparable verb. Be- + gründen (to found).\n'Begründen Sie Ihre These.'

A: Can you justify your opinion?
B: Yes, I justify it on the basis of the data.
A: Well justified!
B: You must be able to justify everything.
'Be-' + 'gründen' (to found). Every claim in German academia must be begründet (justified). No argument without Begründung. The noun is die Begründung.
to argue (academically)
Regular verb. Not fighting, but building\na logical case. 'Überzeugend argumentieren.'

A: How does the author argue?
B: He argues logically and factually.
A: Convincingly argued?
B: Yes, the argumentation is sound.
From Latin 'argumentari.' Building a logical case, not fighting. 'Überzeugend argumentieren' (to argue convincingly). 'Wie argumentiert der Autor?' is a standard exam question.
to discuss, to examine thoroughly
Inseparable verb. Formal academic register.\n'Ein Thema erörtern.'

A: Can we discuss the topic?
B: Yes, let us examine it thoroughly.
A: How does one discuss a topic?
B: Systematically and with evidence.
Old German 'ertörtern' (to explore a place). The Erörterung (discussion essay) is a standard German school format. Every student writes hundreds. Formal register only.
to conclude, to infer
Regular verb. From 'Folge' (consequence).\n'Daraus lässt sich folgern, dass...'

A: What do you conclude from that?
B: I conclude that the hypothesis is correct.
A: Can one conclude that with certainty?
B: Yes, from this one can conclude that...
From 'Folge' (consequence). The academic verb for drawing a conclusion. 'Daraus lässt sich folgern, dass...' (From this one can conclude that...). Related to 'folglich' (consequently).
to draw a conclusion
Inseparable compound verb.\nSchluss (conclusion) + folgern (to infer).

A: What can we conclude?
B: We can conclude that language changes.
A: An important conclusion.
B: Yes, we draw conclusions on the basis of the data.
'Schluss' (conclusion) + 'folgern' (to infer). Inseparable compound verb. Combines the finality of 'Schluss' with the logic of 'folgern.' The noun: Schlussfolgerung.
to reflect
Regular verb. From Latin 'reflectere.'\n'Kritisch reflektieren' (to reflect critically).

A: Have you reflected on the lecture?
B: Yes, I always reflect afterwards.
A: What did you reflect on?
B: Whether I truly understood the connections.

From Latin 'reflectere' (to bend back). Academic reflection means looking back at your own argument and questioning it. Self-awareness in scholarship. 'Kritisch reflektieren' is a core academic skill.
abstract
Adjective. The opposite of 'konkret.'\n'Abstrakte Begriffe' (abstract concepts).

A: Is the topic not too abstract?
B: Abstract topics are normal in linguistics.
A: How do you make abstract things concrete?
B: With examples and data.
From Latin 'abstractus' (drawn away). 'Abstrakte Begriffe' like Freiheit, Gerechtigkeit, Wahrheit. Academic thinking moves between abstrakt and konkret. Both are necessary.
logical
Adjective. 'Logisch aufgebaut' (logically structured).\nCasually also means 'Obviously!'

A: Is your argument logical?
B: Yes, logically structured. Step by step.
A: Obviously!
B: Thanks. Thinking logically is the goal.
From Greek 'logikos.' 'Logisch!' also means 'Obviously!' in casual German. The academic meaning demands rigorous step-by-step reasoning. Two registers, one word.
relevant
Adjective. 'Relevante Literatur.'\nProfessors love asking: 'Ist das relevant?'

A: Is this article relevant?
B: Yes, very relevant for our research.
A: Why is it relevant?
B: Because it explains the connection.
From Latin 'relevare' (to raise up). German professors love asking: 'Ist das relevant?' If you cannot answer yes with evidence, cut it from your paper.
objective, factual, matter-of-fact
Adjective. From 'Sache' (thing/matter).\n'Bleiben Sie sachlich' (Stick to the facts).

A: Was the discussion factual?
B: Yes, everyone stayed objective.
A: Discussing factually is hard.
B: But important. Factual, not emotional.
From 'Sache' (thing/matter). Academic register demands sachlich tone. No emotions, no drama, just evidence. 'Bleiben Sie sachlich' is a command every German student hears.
essential, substantial
Adjective. From 'Wesen' (essence/being).\n'Ein wesentlicher Unterschied.'

A: Is there an essential difference?
B: Yes, an essential difference lies in the method.
A: Is that substantial?
B: Yes, substantial for the result.

From 'Wesen' (essence/being). What touches the essence of the matter. More philosophical than 'wichtig' (important). 'Ein wesentlicher Unterschied' carries more weight than 'ein wichtiger Unterschied.'
consequently, therefore
Adverb. Folge (consequence) + -lich.\nAcademic connector. Signals a conclusion.

A: The data are clear. Consequently...
B: Consequently we must adjust our thesis.
A: Consequently the first hypothesis was wrong?
B: Yes, consequently we need a new one.
From 'Folge' (consequence) + '-lich.' 'Folglich müssen wir annehmen, dass...' (Consequently we must assume that...). Signals that a conclusion follows from evidence.
would have (Konjunktiv II of haben)
Konjunktiv II of 'haben'. Used in hypothetical past.\n'Hätte' + Partizip II = something that didn't happen.

A: Would you have read the text?
B: Had I had time, I would have read it.
A: And now?
B: Now it is too late. Had I known earlier...
Hätte is the Konjunktiv II of haben. In the hypothetical past (Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit), hätte + Partizip II expresses what could have happened but did not. Paired with wäre for sein-verbs.

The Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit expresses hypothetical events in the past: things that COULD have happened but did NOT.

Formation uses two auxiliaries, each paired with a Partizip II:
- hätte: used with most verbs (the same ones that take haben in the Perfekt). 'Hätte ich das gewusst!' (Had I known that!)
- wäre: used with movement verbs, change-of-state verbs, and sein itself. 'Wäre ich früher gekommen...' (Had I come earlier...)

Example: 'Hätte man früher geforscht, wäre das Ergebnis anders gewesen.' (Had they researched earlier, the result would have been different.)

The Partizip II always goes at the end of the clause.

Hätte is used with verbs that take 'haben' in the Perfekt: Ich habe gemacht > Ich hätte gemacht. Wäre is used with verbs that take 'sein' in the Perfekt: Ich bin gegangen > Ich wäre gegangen. The rule is the same as for the Perfekt. Motion verbs (gehen, kommen, fahren), changes of state (werden, sterben, einschlafen), and 'sein' itself use wäre. Everything else uses hätte.
Hätte for verbs that take 'haben' in the Perfekt. Wäre for verbs that take 'sein' in the Perfekt.
'Hätte' + Partizip II signals something that could have happened but did not.
A regret about the past: 'If only I had...' Which Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit auxiliary do verbs using haben take?
Verumius thinks: 'If only I had read the text beforehand!'
Ninety minutes. Sixteen pages of notes, eight of them illegible. The professor did not look at the slides once. He recited them from memory. I am in the right place.
Master the art of turning verbs into nouns and nouns into verbs. Understand why academic German is so noun-heavy. Learn evaluation vocabulary.
Master the patterns of Nominalisierung (-ung, -heit, -keit)
Understand Verbalisierung (noun/adjective to verb)
Learn 20 words for evaluation and critical thinking
the improvement
Feminine noun. From 'verbessern' (to improve).\nClassic -ung Nominalisierung.

A: Is there an improvement?
B: Yes, a clear improvement in the results.
A: What does the improvement consist of?
B: The method is now more precise.
From 'verbessern' (to improve). The -ung suffix transforms any verb into a feminine noun. 'Die Verbesserung der Methode' = 'dass man die Methode verbessert.' Same meaning, different density.
the deterioration
Feminine noun. From 'verschlechtern' (to worsen).\nExact opposite of Verbesserung.

A: Is there a deterioration?
B: Unfortunately yes, a deterioration in quality.
A: Since when the deterioration?
B: The deterioration began two months ago.
From 'verschlechtern' (to worsen). The exact opposite of Verbesserung. German loves these pairs: besser/schlechter becomes Verbesserung/Verschlechterung. Symmetry in language.
the explanation, the declaration
Feminine noun. From 'erklären' (to explain).\nAlso: Steuererklärung (tax return).

A: Do you have an explanation for that?
B: Yes, my explanation is the following...
A: A good explanation.
B: Every thesis needs an explanation.
From 'erklären' (to explain). Also the word for a tax return: Steuererklärung. Explaining yourself to the Finanzamt uses the same word as explaining your thesis.
the justification, the reasoning
Feminine noun. From 'begründen' (to justify).\n'Ohne Begründung' = academic death sentence.

A: What is your justification?
B: My justification is based on the data.
A: Without justification it does not work.
B: Every claim needs a justification.
From 'begründen' (to justify). 'Ohne Begründung' is the academic death sentence. Every claim needs its Begründung. No argument survives without one.
the evaluation, the assessment
Feminine noun. From 'bewerten' (to evaluate).\n'Die Bewertung der Ergebnisse.'

A: How does the evaluation turn out?
B: The evaluation is positive.
A: Who does the evaluation?
B: The professor gives the evaluation.
From 'bewerten' (to evaluate). Your Hausarbeit gets a Bewertung. Your professor gives it a Note (grade). The Bewertung determines your academic fate.
the observation
Feminine noun. From 'beobachten' (to observe).\n'Empirische Beobachtungen.'

A: What do your observations show?
B: The observation shows a clear pattern.
A: How many observations have you made?
B: Over a hundred observations.
From 'beobachten' (to observe). The foundation of all science: first you beobachten, then you analysieren. 'Empirische Beobachtungen' are the raw material of research.
the solution
Feminine noun. From 'lösen' (to solve).\nAlso: liquid solution in chemistry.

A: Have you found a solution?
B: Yes, a creative solution.
A: What is the solution?
B: The solution lies in the method.
From 'lösen' (to solve/dissolve). Also means a liquid solution in chemistry. Solving is dissolving the problem. 'Eine kreative Lösung finden' is what every researcher aims for.
the decision
Feminine noun. From 'entscheiden' (to decide).\n'Eine wichtige Entscheidung treffen.'

A: Have you made the decision?
B: Yes, the decision is final.
A: Was it a difficult decision?
B: Yes, but the right decision.
Ent- (un-/dis-) + scheiden (to separate). A decision separates one path from another. You cut away alternatives until one remains. 'Eine wichtige Entscheidung treffen' is a core phrase.
the ability, the skill
Feminine noun. Fähig (capable) + -keit.\nThe -keit suffix works on adjectives ending in -ig.

A: What skills do you need?
B: Analytical skills are most important.
A: Do you have this ability?
B: I am working on improving this ability.
From 'fähig' (capable) + '-keit.' The -keit suffix works on adjectives ending in -ig. 'Analytische Fähigkeiten' (analytical skills) are required on every German academic CV. Always feminine.
the gratitude
Feminine noun. Dankbar (grateful) + -keit.\nAbstract qualities love the -keit ending.

A: Do you feel gratitude?
B: Yes, great gratitude for this chance.
A: Gratitude is important.
B: Without gratitude you forget where you come from.
'Dankbar' (grateful) + '-keit.' Another -keit nominalization. The adjective 'dankbar' becomes the abstract noun 'Dankbarkeit.' A pattern that creates entire families of abstract nouns.
to evaluate, to assign value
Inseparable verb. Be- + Wert (value).\n'Wie bewerten Sie dieses Ergebnis?'

A: How do you evaluate the result?
B: I evaluate it positively.
A: Can one evaluate it objectively?
B: One must evaluate it against the criteria.
'Be-' + 'Wert' (value). Literally to assign value. The reverse of Bewertung: verb from noun root. 'Wie bewerten Sie dieses Ergebnis?' is the professor's standard question.
to judge, to assess
Inseparable verb. Be- + Urteil (judgment).\nSlightly different from bewerten.

A: How do you assess the situation?
B: I assess it cautiously.
A: Can one judge it fairly?
B: One must assess all sides.
'Be-' + 'Urteil' (judgment). Slightly different from bewerten: beurteilen is about judgment, bewerten about assigning value. 'Die Qualität beurteilen' (to assess the quality).
to evaluate, to analyze (data)
Separable verb. Aus (out) + werten (to value).\n'Daten auswerten' (to analyze data).

A: Have you analyzed the data yet?
B: Yes, I am currently analyzing the last data.
A: What does the analysis show?
B: The analysis confirms the hypothesis.
'Aus' (out) + 'werten' (to value). You extract the value from raw data. Separable: 'Ich werte die Daten aus.' The nominalized form: die Auswertung (the data analysis).
to find out, to discover
Separable verb. Heraus (out) + finden (to find).\n'Wir wollen herausfinden, ob...'

A: What do you want to find out?
B: I want to find out whether the thesis is correct.
A: And? Have you found out?
B: Yes, I finally found it out.
'Heraus' (out) + 'finden' (to find). The verb of research curiosity. Separable. Strong verb: herausfinden, fand heraus, herausgefunden. Finding what was hidden.
to verify, to review
Inseparable verb. Über (over) + prüfen (to test).\n'Die Hypothese überprüfen.'

A: Have you verified the sources?
B: Yes, I double-check everything.
A: Why verify?
B: Because you must always verify results.
'Über' (over) + 'prüfen' (to test/check). Not separable. Checking something thoroughly by going over it. 'Überprüfung' is the nominalized form.
to question critically, to challenge
Inseparable verb. Hinter (behind) + fragen (ask).\n'Ergebnisse hinterfragen.'

A: Should one question everything?
B: Yes, questioning critically is important.
A: Even your own results?
B: Especially your own results you must question.
'Hinter' (behind) + 'fragen' (to ask). Asking what is BEHIND the surface. Not separable. The hallmark of critical thinking in German academia.
critical (positive: thoughtful)
Adjective. In academia, kritisch is POSITIVE.\n'Kritisches Denken' (critical thinking).

A: Are you critical enough?
B: I try to always be critical.
A: Being critical is a virtue.
B: Yes, critical thinking is the goal.
From Greek 'kritikos' (able to judge). In German academia, kritisch is positive: it means thoughtful, not negative. 'Seien Sie kritisch' means 'think carefully,' not 'be negative.'
precise, exact
Adjective. From Latin 'praecisus' (cut short).\n'Präzise formulieren' (to formulate precisely).

A: Is that precise enough?
B: No, formulate it more precisely.
A: How precise must it be?
B: As precise as possible.
From Latin 'praecisus' (cut short). Academic writing demands precision. Vague language is the enemy. 'Seien Sie präziser' is a common margin note from German professors.
comprehensible, traceable
Adjective. Nach (after) + vollziehbar (traceable).\n'Ihre Argumentation muss nachvollziehbar sein.'

A: Is the argumentation comprehensible?
B: Yes, every step is traceable.
A: Comprehensible for laypeople?
B: Also comprehensible for laypeople.
'Nach' (after) + 'vollziehbar' (executable/traceable). Others must be able to follow your logic step by step. If it is not nachvollziehbar, it is not wissenschaftlich.
methodical
Adjective. Methode + -isch.\n'Methodisch vorgehen' (to proceed methodically).

A: Are you proceeding methodically?
B: Yes, always methodically. Structure is everything.
A: Methodical work is mandatory.
B: Without a methodical approach, no result.
From 'Methode' + '-isch.' German academia demands methodisches Arbeiten (methodical work) as a baseline, not a bonus. Unmethodisch is never acceptable.

German builds nouns from verbs and adjectives using predictable suffixes. All resulting nouns are feminine.

The -ung suffix turns verbs into nouns:
- erklären becomes Erklärung (explanation)
- beobachten becomes Beobachtung (observation)
- bewerten becomes Bewertung (evaluation)

The -heit suffix turns adjectives into nouns:
- frei becomes Freiheit (freedom)
- sicher becomes Sicherheit (security)

Adjectives ending in -ig, -lich, or -bar take -keit instead:
- fähig becomes Fähigkeit (ability)
- dankbar becomes Dankbarkeit (gratitude)

Verbalization works in the opposite direction, turning nouns and adjectives into verbs:
- Analyse becomes analysieren, klar becomes klären, Kritik becomes kritisieren

Academic German prefers the nominal style. Spoken German prefers the verbal style.

The nominal style compresses: 'Die Verbesserung der Methode führte zur Optimierung der Ergebnisse.' The verbal style unpacks: 'Weil man die Methode verbessert hat, konnte man die Ergebnisse optimieren.' Both say the same thing. The nominal style saves words, sounds more formal, but is harder to read. The verbal style is clearer but longer. Good academic texts mix both styles.
'Hätte ... gewirkt' and 'wäre ... ausgefallen' are hypothetical past. 'Begründung' and 'Bewertung' are nominalized forms.
the evaluation, the analysis
Nominalization of auswerten. The -ung suffix always creates feminine nouns.
The evaluation of the data takes two weeks.
German loves turning verbs into nouns with -ung. It is one of the most productive word-formation patterns in the language.
Nominalize the verb 'auswerten' using the most productive German noun-forming suffix.
The evaluation of the results took three months.
Both blanks are infinitive verbs. One expresses qualitative judgment, the other expresses critical examination from behind the surface.
The Mensa: Schnitzel for 2.50 EUR and new friendships. Learn vocabulary for student life, practice Konjunktiv II past in conversation, and chat with fellow students.
Learn 20 new words about student life and exams
Practice Konjunktiv II past in casual conversations
Discuss academic life with fellow students
The Mensa. Long tables, the clatter of trays, the smell of Schnitzel for 2.50 EUR. Verumius looks at his tray. Schnitzel, potato salad, a drink. Total: 3.20 EUR. He blinks.
This costs only 3.20? In Istanbul I would not have even gotten a coffee for that.
He sits down at a long table. A student introduces herself. 'Hi, I am Jana. Linguistics, third semester. And you?'
Verumius. Also linguistics. First semester. Had I come to Germany earlier, I would have started sooner.
the semester break
Always plural. Semester + Ferien (holidays).\n'In den Semesterferien' (during semester break).

A: What are you doing during semester break?
B: I am writing my term paper.
A: That does not sound like a holiday.
B: During semester break you are never truly free.
German students call them 'vorlesungsfreie Zeit' (lecture-free time), but they are NOT free: exam preparation, internships, and Hausarbeiten fill every day. The name is misleading by design.
the scholarship
Neuter noun. Plural: Stipendien.\nFrom Latin 'stipendium' (tax/payment).

A: Do you have a scholarship?
B: Yes, the Deutschlandstipendium.
A: How much do you get?
B: 300 EUR per month. Not much, but it helps.

Germany offers Stipendien from 13 major foundations. The Deutschlandstipendium gives 300 EUR per month. Not enough to live on, but prestigious on a CV. Half comes from the government, half from private donors.
the student dormitory
Neuter noun. Studenten + Wohn (living) + Heim (home).\nA triple compound noun.

A: Do you live in the student dormitory?
B: Yes, on the third floor.
A: How is it?
B: Small, but the rent is affordable.
Rooms cost 200-400 EUR per month. Shared kitchen, shared bathroom, shared everything. A German student rite of passage. Waiting lists can be months long. The Studentenwerk manages most Wohnheime.
the exam, the test
Feminine noun. From 'prüfen' (to test).\n'Eine Prüfung bestehen' (to pass an exam).

A: When is your next exam?
B: In two weeks.
A: Are you prepared?
B: Not yet. I still have a lot to study.

German university exams can be oral (mündlich) or written (schriftlich). Failing twice at some universities means you cannot repeat the course. The Prüfungsangst (exam anxiety) is real and even has its own Wikipedia page.
the written exam
Feminine noun. From Latin 'clausura' (enclosure).\nAlways written, always timed.

A: How did the written exam go?
B: Hard. Three hours, five questions.
A: Did you finish everything?
B: Almost. The last question was too complex.

Different from Prüfung: a Klausur is always written, always timed, always in a supervised room. 'Klausur schreiben' (to take a written exam). The word comes from Latin 'clausura' (enclosure), because you are locked in.
the term paper, the research paper
Feminine noun. Haus (home) + Arbeit (work).\nTypically 15-25 pages with strict formatting.

A: Have you started the term paper yet?
B: No, I am still looking for a research question.
A: The deadline is in four weeks.
B: I know. The pressure is growing.

Written at home, hence the name. Typically 15-25 pages. German Hausarbeiten have strict formatting rules: margins, font size, citation style, everything regulated. The Deckblatt (cover page) alone has rules.
the fellow student (male)
Masculine noun. N-Deklination: Kommilitone, Kommilitonen.\nFrom Latin 'commilito' (fellow soldier).

A: Do you know many fellow students?
B: Yes, there are nice fellow students in my seminar.
A: Do you work together?
B: Sometimes. We form study groups.

Students as 'comrades-in-arms' in the battle of education. N-Deklination: den Kommilitonen, dem Kommilitonen. Only used for university students, not school pupils. The word carries a sense of shared struggle.
the fellow student (female)
Feminine noun. Kommilitone + '-in.'\nPlural: Kommilitoninnen.

A: Jana is my fellow student.
B: Does she also study linguistics?
A: Yes, third semester.
B: An experienced fellow student then.

The feminine form follows the standard '-in' pattern. The gender-neutral trend uses 'Kommiliton*innen' or 'Mitstudierende' (fellow students). But Kommilitone/Kommilitonin persists in everyday university language.
the office hours
Feminine noun. Sprech (speaking) + Stunde (hour).\n'In die Sprechstunde gehen' (to go to office hours).

A: Have you been to office hours yet?
B: Yes, last week at the professor's.
A: Did he help you?
B: Yes, he discussed my outline.

Every German professor holds Sprechstunden. You must usually sign up in advance on a list outside the office door. 'Sprechstunde' is also used for doctors. The concept: a designated hour for speaking.
the examination regulations
Feminine noun. Prüfung + Ordnung (order/regulation).\nThe bureaucratic rulebook for all exams.

A: Have you read the examination regulations?
B: Yes, it is 40 pages long.
A: 40 pages?
B: German thoroughness. Every rule is in there.
Every Studiengang has one. It specifies how many exam attempts, what percentage to pass, which deadlines. German Ordnung at its finest. Students who do not read their Prüfungsordnung regret it.
to submit
Separable verb. Ein (in) + reichen (to hand/reach).\n'Die Hausarbeit einreichen' (to submit the paper).

A: When do you have to submit the paper?
B: By next Friday.
A: Will you manage?
B: I must. A deadline is a deadline.

You 'reach it in' to the professor or department. Missing the Einreichungsfrist (submission deadline) typically means zero points. No exceptions, no extensions. German academic culture values punctuality above almost everything.
to specialize
Regular verb. Reflexive: 'sich auf etwas spezialisieren.'\nFrom Latin 'specialis.'

A: What do you specialize in?
B: I specialize in computational linguistics.
A: That is an exciting field.
B: Yes, the specialization is fun for me.

Reflexive with 'sich auf.' From the general to the specific. 'Spezialisierung' is the nominalized form. German universities encourage early Spezialisierung compared to the broader American liberal arts model.
to give a presentation, to present
Regular -ieren verb. 'Über ein Thema referieren.'\nA Referat is a student presentation.

A: Who is presenting next week?
B: Me. About nominalization.
A: Are you nervous?
B: A little. My first presentation in German.

A Referat is a student presentation, typically 15-20 minutes with a handout. 'Wer referiert nächsste Woche?' is the professor's dreaded question. The word comes from Latin 'referre' (to report). Academic ritual.
to publish
Regular -ieren verb.\n'Einen Artikel publizieren' (to publish an article).

A: Have you published anything yet?
B: Not yet. Only after the Master's.
A: The professor has published a lot.
B: Yes, over 50 articles. That is impressive.

The academic pressure: 'Publish or perish' is 'Publiziere oder gehe unter' in German. Publications are academic currency. Your Publikationsliste (list of publications) determines your career. Quantity and quality both count.
to earn a doctorate, to do a PhD
Regular -ieren verb.\n'Sie promoviert in Physik' (she is doing her PhD in physics).

A: Would you like to do a PhD?
B: Maybe. First finish the Master's.
A: How long does a doctorate take?
B: Three to five years, depending on the department.

German doctoral titles (Dr.) are used in everyday life, even on doorbells and ID cards. 'Herr Doktor' is a form of address. The Promotion (doctorate) is the highest academic achievement, and Germans wear it proudly.
reliable
Adjective. Zu (to) + verlaessig (relying).\n'Zuverlässiige Quellen' (reliable sources).

A: Is Wikipedia a reliable source?
B: For a starting point yes, but not as the only one.
A: What is more reliable?
B: Academic journals and primary sources.

Academic writing demands zuverlässiige sources. Wikipedia alone is never zuverlässiig enough for a Hausarbeit. The adjective also describes people: 'Er ist zuverlässiig' (He is reliable). A high compliment in German culture.
well-founded, substantiated
Adjective/participle. From Latin 'fundare' (to found).\n'Fundierte Argumentation' (well-founded argumentation).

A: How must a good thesis be?
B: Well-founded. Every claim needs evidence.
A: What does 'fundiert' mean exactly?
B: Built on a solid foundation.

Your claims need a Fundament (foundation). A fundierte Studie is one built on solid evidence. The opposite: 'unbefundiert' or 'haltlos' (baseless). In German academia, unfundierte claims are the cardinal sin.
complex
Adjective. From Latin 'complexus' (intertwined).\n'Ein komplexes Thema' (a complex topic).

A: The research question is quite complex.
B: Yes, but complexity is no reason to give up.
A: How do you deal with it?
B: I break the complex into simple parts.

Academic code for 'this will be difficult.' 'Die Komplexitaet' (the complexity) is the nominalized form. German scholars love to call problems 'komplex' rather than 'schwierig.' It sounds more respectful of the challenge.
sound, valid (of arguments)
Adjective. Stich (stab) + haltig (holding).\n'Ein stichhaltiges Argument' (a sound argument).

A: Is the argument sound?
B: Yes, it withstands any criticism.
A: How do you know?
B: I checked it from three perspectives.

Literally: an argument that 'holds up under stabbing.' Battle-tested logic. If your Argument is stichhaltig, no one can poke holes in it. The metaphor is beautifully physical for something purely intellectual.
comprehensive
Adjective/participle. Um (around) + fassend (grasping).\n'Eine umfassende Studie' (a comprehensive study).

A: How thorough was the investigation?
B: Very comprehensive. All aspects were considered.
A: That is impressive.
B: Comprehensive research takes time.

Grasping something from all sides. 'Umfassend' means nothing is left out. The prefix 'um-' (around) creates the image of wrapping your arms around an entire topic. The ultimate academic compliment for a study.
'Hätte ... begonnen' and 'wäre ... eingetreten' are hypothetical past. 'Forschung' and 'Verbesserung' are nominalized verbs.
Kommen uses sein in the Perfekt. Which Konjunktiv II Vergangenheit auxiliary do sein-verbs take?
Verumius says: 'Had I come to Germany earlier, I would have started sooner.'
After the class, the written assignment must be submitted. Two university-specific nouns.
Verumius pushes the empty tray back. 3.20 EUR for a warm meal, new acquaintances, and the feeling of belonging. The Mensa is not a restaurant. It is an institution.
Jana gave me the tip: the library on the third floor has the best workspaces. Had I not met her in the Mensa, I would not know that. This is how university works: you learn on the side.

Verumius meets Yilmaz in the university library. His advisor helps with the Hausarbeit. Learn vocabulary for academic writing, practice all three grammar constructions, and understand German citation rules.
Learn 20 new words for academic writing
Practice Konjunktiv II past, Nominalisierung, and Verbalisierung
Understand German citation and plagiarism rules

University library. Reading room with long wooden tables and green reading lamps. Books pile up around Verumius. Yilmaz sits across from him. Their relationship has evolved: from teacher to advisor, from mentor to near-equal.
Verumius, your outline is good. But the introduction needs a clearer research question. And do not forget the footnotes.
Had I known how strict the rules are, I would have started the research earlier.
Every claim needs proof. Every quote needs a source citation. No plagiarism. That is the foundation of academic work.
the diploma thesis
Feminine noun. Diplom + Arbeit.\nThe old German degree thesis before the Bologna reform.

A: Did your father write a diploma thesis?
B: Yes, in engineering.
A: Does the diploma still exist?
B: No, now there is Bachelor's and Master's.

Many Germans over 40 still proudly cite their Diplomarbeit. The Bologna reform replaced the Diplom with Bachelor and Master around 2010. The Diplom-Ingenieur (Dipl.-Ing.) title is a source of generational pride.
the doctoral thesis
Feminine noun. From Latin 'dissertatio' (discussion).\n'An der Dissertation schreiben' (to be writing one's doctoral thesis).

A: How long does one write a doctoral thesis?
B: Three to seven years.
A: That long?
B: Yes, a dissertation is a book.

German Dissertationen are published as books, often by academic publishers. Writing one can last 3-7 years. The Rigorosum or Disputation (oral defense) is the final hurdle. Several German politicians lost their Dr. titles due to Dissertations-Plagiate.
the bibliography
Neuter noun. Literatur + Verzeichnis (index/list).\nAlphabetically ordered list of all sources.

A: How many sources are in your bibliography?
B: Over thirty.
A: That is a lot.
B: Every single source must be verifiable.

Every German Hausarbeit ends with one. Alphabetically ordered by author last name. Missing a source equals academic dishonesty. The Literaturverzeichnis is the proof that you did your homework. Literally.
the footnote
Feminine noun. Fuss (foot) + Note (note).\nGerman academic writing uses footnotes extensively.

A: How many footnotes does your paper have?
B: Already over fifty.
A: Fifty footnotes?
B: Yes, every claim needs a footnote.

Some pages in German academic writing have more footnote text than main text. Fussnoten prove you did your research. They contain citations, cross-references, and additional commentary. A Hausarbeit without Fussnoten is incomplete.
the introduction
Feminine noun. Ein (in) + Leitung (leading).\n'In der Einleitung stellen Sie Ihre These vor.'

A: What belongs in the introduction?
B: The research question, the method, the outline.
A: And the thesis?
B: Yes, the thesis comes at the end of the introduction.

The Einleitung leads the reader into your argument. It must contain: Forschungsfrage, Methode, Gliederung, and your central These. A good Einleitung is a contract with the reader about what they will receive.
the outline, the structure
Feminine noun. From 'gliedern' (to structure).\nNumbered: 1., 1.1, 1.1.1, etc.

A: Do you have an outline yet?
B: Yes, three main chapters with subchapters.
A: Show me the outline.
B: Here: introduction, main body, conclusion.

German Hausarbeiten require a detailed Gliederung with numbered sections: 1., 1.1, 1.1.1. The Gliederung IS the argument in miniature. Professors often ask to see the Gliederung before you start writing.
the proof, the evidence
Masculine noun. Nach (after) + Weis (showing).\n'Einen Nachweis erbringen' (to provide proof).

A: Do you have proof for this claim?
B: Yes, three studies support it.
A: Good. Nothing works without proof.
B: I have learned that.

Academic and bureaucratic German. Your Leistungsnachweis proves you passed a course. Your Sprachnachweis proves your language level. Germany loves Nachweise: written proof for everything. Without a Nachweis, it did not happen.
the plagiarism
Neuter noun. From Latin 'plagiarius' (kidnapper).\nGerman universities scan every submission.

A: What happens with plagiarism?
B: You fail. In the worst case: expulsion.
A: That strict?
B: Yes. Plagiarism is a serious offense in Germany.

German universities take Plagiate extremely seriously. Software scans every submission. Several German politicians lost their doctoral titles to Plagiat scandals, including a former defense minister. The word comes from Latin for 'kidnapper.'
the research, the investigation
Feminine noun. From French 'recherche' (search).\nMore specific than Forschung.

A: How far along are you with the research?
B: Almost done. I found all sources.
A: Thorough research is half the work.
B: That is true. The research took weeks.

More specific than Forschung: Recherche is the active search for information. Forschung is the broader scientific endeavor. Journalists do Recherche too. 'Gruendliche Recherche' (thorough research) is the prerequisite for everything academic.
the order, the sequence
Feminine noun. Reihen (rows) + Folge (sequence).\n'In der richtigen Reihenfolge' (in the correct order).

A: In what order do I write the chapters?
B: Introduction last. First the main body.
A: Really? The introduction last?
B: Yes, that way you know the right sequence.

The order in which things follow in a row. German loves compound precision. 'In chronologischer Reihenfolge' (in chronological order), 'in alphabetischer Reihenfolge' (in alphabetical order). The concept of order has its own compound word.
to write, to author
Regular verb. Ver- + fassen (to grasp/compose).\nMore formal than 'schreiben.'

A: How long did it take you to write the text?
B: Three weeks for the first draft.
A: And then?
B: Then I revised it.

More formal than 'schreiben.' You verfassen a Hausarbeit. You schreiben a WhatsApp message. Academic register demands 'verfassen' for serious texts. 'Der Verfasser' (the author) is the formal word for who wrote it.
to paraphrase
Regular -ieren verb.\nRestating ideas in your own words.

A: May I quote the author directly?
B: Yes, but it is better to paraphrase.
A: What is the difference?
B: When paraphrasing you reformulate the idea.

Essential academic skill: restating ideas in your own words while keeping the meaning. Bad paraphrasieren too close to the original equals Plagiat. German professors can spot lazy paraphrasing instantly.
to quote, to cite
Regular -ieren verb. From Latin 'citare' (to summon).\n'Korrekt zitieren' (to cite correctly).

A: How do I cite correctly?
B: Name, year, page number in the footnote.
A: And in the bibliography?
B: There goes the complete source citation.

German citation styles (Chicago, Harvard, APA) are taught in every Proseminar. 'Wer nicht zitiert, plagiiert' (Who does not cite, plagiarizes). The Zitat (quote) must be exact. Even a missing comma can be a problem.
to reconstruct
Regular -ieren verb. Re- (again) + konstruieren.\n'Ein Argument rekonstruieren' (to reconstruct an argument).

A: Can you reconstruct the author's argument?
B: Yes. He argues in three steps.
A: Which ones?
B: Thesis, justification, conclusion.

Building something again from its pieces. Academic skill: understanding HOW an argument was built. 'Die Rekonstruktion' is the nominalized form. In Hausarbeiten you often must reconstruct other scholars' arguments before critiquing them.
to revise, to rework
Inseparable verb. Über (over) + arbeiten (to work).\n'Den Text überarbeiten' (to revise the text).

A: How often did you revise the text?
B: Three times. The first draft is never good enough.
A: That sounds like a lot of work.
B: Revision is the most important step.

Not separable. Working OVER your text again and again. First draft is never good enough. Die Überarbeitung is where quality lives. German professors expect multiple drafts. 'Überarbeiten Sie den Text' is the most common feedback.
to prove, to demonstrate
Separable verb. Nach (after) + weisen (to show).\nStronger than 'zeigen.'

A: Can this thesis be proven?
B: Yes, the data clearly demonstrate it.
A: How?
B: Through statistical analysis.

Stronger than 'zeigen' (to show): nachweisen demands evidence. 'Ich weise nach, dass...' (I demonstrate that...). The nominalized form 'der Nachweis' is equally important. Academic German distinguishes between showing and proving.
to refer, to cite
Inseparable verb. Ver- + weisen (to point).\n'Auf eine Quelle verweisen' (to refer to a source).

A: Which source does the author refer to?
B: He refers to a study from 2019.
A: Is the study reliable?
B: Yes, it was published in an academic journal.
Not separable. 'Siehe auch...' (See also...) is a Verweis in written form. 'Der Verweis' (the reference) is the noun. Academic texts are networks of Verweise: every claim points to another source.
to deepen, to explore in depth
Inseparable verb. Ver- + tiefen (from tief, deep).\nVerbalisierung: adjective 'tief' becomes verb 'vertiefen.'

A: Would you like to explore the topic in depth?
B: Yes, I want to deepen the analysis.
A: In which direction?
B: I want to deepen the theoretical framework.

Verbalisierung in action: the adjective 'tief' (deep) becomes the verb 'vertiefen' (to deepen). Going below the surface. 'Die Vertiefung' is the nominalized form. Academic work IS Vertiefung: going deeper than the surface allows.
analytical
Adjective. From Greek 'analytikos.'\n'Analytisches Denken' (analytical thinking).

A: What does analytical thinking mean?
B: Breaking problems into parts and solving them systematically.
A: Is that important in studies?
B: It is the most important skill.

The adjective form of Analyse. 'Ein analytischer Ansatz' (an analytical approach). Required on every German academic CV under 'Faehigkeiten' (skills). Analytical thinking is not just valued: it is expected as baseline competence.
significant
Adjective. From Latin 'significare' (to indicate).\n'Statistisch signifikant' (statistically significant).

A: Are the results significant?
B: Yes, statistically significant.
A: What does that mean exactly?
B: The result is very probably not a coincidence.

In research, signifikant has a precise meaning: the result is unlikely to be random (p < 0.05). Not just 'important' but 'proven meaningful.' Using 'signifikant' casually is a common mistake that annoys statisticians.
'Hätte ... nachgewiesen' and 'wäre ... ausgefallen' are hypothetical past. 'Recherche', 'Verbesserung', 'Schlussfolgerung' are all nominalized forms.
The section at the end of a paper where all cited sources are listed alphabetically.
All sources used are listed alphabetically in the bibliography.
to research, to investigate
From French 'rechercher'. Active verb for finding sources.\nMore specific than 'forschen': targeted source search.

A: Have you researched the topic well?
B: Yes, I searched three databases.
A: How long did you research?
B: Five hours. Thorough research takes time.

Borrowed from French 'rechercher' in the 18th century. More specific than forschen: you recherchieren when actively hunting for sources. Journalists and academics both recherchieren. The noun is 'die Recherche.'
Two academic verbs: first you search for sources, then you evaluate the results. Both are examples of verbalization.
Verumius, one last thing. The term paper is not the goal. It is the tool. You are learning to follow a thought to its conclusion. That is the real skill.

Ten PM. The library closes. Verumius packs his books. The outline is done. The research question is formulated. Had someone told him a year ago that he would write an academic paper in German, he would have taken it for a bad joke.
Test everything from Unit 25. Prove you have mastered academic German, can use Konjunktiv II past, and switch between nominal and verbal style.

Early morning, Dahlem. The campus lies quiet. Verumius sits in the empty library. In front of him: flashcards, notes, highlighted text passages. Today is the day that shows whether the last weeks have left their mark.
the research question
Feminine noun. Forschung + Frage.\nThe first thing every Hausarbeit must state.

A: What is your research question?
B: Does nominalization affect comprehensibility?
A: That is a clear research question.
B: Without a research question, no direction.

The first thing every Hausarbeit must state. 'Was ist Ihre Forschungsfrage?' Without one, your paper has no direction. A good Forschungsfrage is specific, answerable, and relevant. It is the compass of your entire paper.
the research result
Neuter noun. Forschung + Ergebnis.\nPlural: Forschungsergebnisse.

A: What are the most important research results?
B: Nominalization reduces readability by 30 percent.
A: A clear research result.
B: Yes, the data are unambiguous.

A compound built from two already-nominalized words: Forschung (from forschen) + Ergebnis (from ergeben). Plural: Forschungsergebnisse. German loves stacking nominalizations. Each layer adds precision.
the department, the disciplinary area
Masculine noun. Fach (subject) + Bereich (area).\n'Im Fachbereich Linguistik.'

A: In which department do you study?
B: In the department of linguistics and literature.
A: That is a large department.
B: Yes, with over 2000 students.

More specific than Fakultaet. A Fachbereich is a subdivision of a Fakultaet. 'Im Fachbereich Linguistik' (in the linguistics department). The organizational unit where your academic life happens daily.
the field of expertise
Neuter noun. Fach + Gebiet (territory).\n'Mein Fachgebiet ist Computerlinguistik.'

A: What is your field of expertise?
B: Computational linguistics.
A: A modern field.
B: Yes, it connects language and technology.

Your academic territory. Fach (subject) + Gebiet (territory). 'Fachgebiet' implies ownership: this is YOUR area of knowledge. German academics identify strongly with their Fachgebiet. It becomes part of their identity.
the technical language, the jargon
Feminine noun. Fach + Sprache.\nEvery discipline has its own Fachsprache.

A: The technical language of medicine is difficult.
B: Yes, many Latin terms.
A: Every field has its own technical language.
B: Linguists even study technical language itself.

Every academic discipline has its own Fachsprache. Linguists study Fachsprache itself: meta-language about language. 'Medizinische Fachsprache' (medical jargon), 'juristische Fachsprache' (legal jargon). Each is a language within a language.
the university, the college
Feminine noun. Hoch (high) + Schule (school).\nBroader than Universitaet.

A: What is the difference between university and Hochschule?
B: Hochschule is the umbrella term.
A: So all universities are Hochschulen?
B: Yes, but not all Hochschulen are universities.

Broader than Universitaet: includes Fachhochschulen (universities of applied sciences). 'Hochschulbildung' (higher education). Germany has over 400 Hochschulen. The word literally means 'high school' but refers to tertiary education, not secondary.
the certificate of achievement
Masculine noun. Leistung (achievement) + Nachweis (proof).\nProof that you passed a course.

A: Do you have all certificates of achievement?
B: I am still missing one.
A: Which one?
B: The certificate for the introductory seminar.

You collect Leistungsnachweise like currency: each one counts toward your degree. Without the right Leistungsnachweise, you cannot register for the final exam. German bureaucracy meets academia in this compound noun.
the office of the university president
Neuter noun. From Latin 'rector' (ruler).\nThe administrative heart of the university.

A: Where is the president's office?
B: In the main building, third floor.
A: What happens there?
B: University policy is decided there.

Where policy is made. The Rektor or Rektorin is the university president. The Rektorat is their office and the administrative body. 'Im Rektorat vorstellig werden' (to appear at the president's office) is usually not a good sign.
the effectiveness
Feminine noun. Wirksam (effective) + -keit.\nNominalisierung: adjective to noun.

A: How do you test the effectiveness of a method?
B: Through empirical investigations.
A: And if the effectiveness is low?
B: Then you change the method.

Nominalisierung: adjective 'wirksam' (effective) + '-keit' becomes the noun 'Wirksamkeit.' The same pattern: freundlich > Freundlichkeit, zuverlässiig > Zuverlässiigkeit. The '-keit' suffix is productive with '-sam' adjectives.
the random sample
Feminine noun. Stich (stab/prick) + Probe (test/sample).\n'Eine repraesentative Stichprobe.'

A: How large is your sample?
B: 200 texts from five departments.
A: Is that representative?
B: Yes, the sample is large enough.

You 'prick' into the data to extract a test portion. A repraesentative Stichprobe must reflect the whole population. Too small, and your results are not signifikant. Too large, and you waste resources. Finding the right Stichprobe is an art.
to apply
Separable verb. An (on) + wenden (to turn).\n'Methoden anwenden' (to apply methods).

A: Which methods do you apply?
B: Qualitative and quantitative methods.
A: Can you apply both at the same time?
B: Yes, that is called mixed methods.

Separable: 'Ich wende die Methode an.' You turn (wenden) knowledge toward (an) a problem. 'Die Anwendung' is the nominalized form. Verbalisierung in reverse: the noun Anwendung became the verb anwenden, or perhaps the other way around.
to set up, to propose
Separable verb. Auf (up) + stellen (to place).\n'Eine Hypothese aufstellen.'

A: Have you proposed a hypothesis yet?
B: Yes, I propose the following hypothesis.
A: And how do you want to test it?
B: Through the analysis of my sample.

You place your idea up for examination. 'Eine These aufstellen' (to put forward a thesis). Separable: 'Ich stelle eine Hypothese auf.' The Aufstellung (setup/proposal) is the nominalized form. Academic German demands that you aufstellen before you beweisen.
to prove with evidence, to take (a course)
Regular verb. Be- + legen (to lay/place).\nDouble meaning in academic contexts.

A: Can you substantiate your thesis?
B: Yes, three studies support it.
A: And which courses are you taking?
B: Semantics and phonology.

Double meaning: 'Eine These belegen' (to substantiate a thesis with evidence) and 'Einen Kurs belegen' (to enroll in a course). The Beleg (proof/receipt) is the nominalized form. In both cases, you 'lay down' something as proof or claim.
to refute, to disprove
Inseparable verb. Wider (against) + legen (to lay).\nThe opposite of belegen.

A: Can the hypothesis be refuted?
B: Yes, if the data speak against it.
A: Is refutation bad?
B: No, refutation is also a result.

The opposite of belegen. You lay evidence AGAINST a claim. Not separable. 'Die Widerlegung' (refutation) is the nominalized form. In good science, a widerlegt hypothesis is just as valuable as a confirmed one.
to summarize
Separable verb. Zusammen (together) + fassen (to grasp).\n'Die Ergebnisse zusammenfassen.'

A: Can you summarize the results?
B: Yes, briefly: nominalization reduces readability.
A: Well summarized.
B: The summary is in the last chapter.

Grasping everything together into one compact form. Separable: 'Ich fasse die Ergebnisse zusammen.' Die Zusammenfassung (summary) is the nominalized form. Every German Hausarbeit ends with a Zusammenfassung. It must be short, precise, and self-contained.
to evaluate
Regular -ieren verb. From French 'evaluer.'\nMore formal than bewerten.

A: How do you evaluate a teaching program?
B: Through surveys and performance data.
A: Who conducts the evaluation?
B: Usually an external committee.

More formal than bewerten. Used in institutional contexts: Lehrevaluation (teaching evaluation) happens every semester at German universities. Students rate their professors, and the results are taken seriously. Sometimes.
to document
Regular -ieren verb. From Latin 'documentare.'\n'Ergebnisse dokumentieren.'

A: Did you document everything?
B: Yes, every step of the research.
A: Why is documentation so important?
B: So others can verify the results.

Every step of research must be documented. Die Dokumentation is the nominalized form. In German academia, undocumented work might as well not exist. 'Sorgfaeltige Dokumentation' (careful documentation) is a research fundamental.
to differentiate
Regular -ieren verb. From Latin 'differentia.'\n'Zwischen Theorie und Praxis differenzieren.'

A: Can one differentiate here?
B: Yes, one must differentiate between correlation and causality.
A: What is the difference?
B: Correlation does not mean causality.

Academic precision: seeing differences others miss. 'Differenzieren zwischen' (to differentiate between). 'Die Differenzierung' is the nominalized form. German academic culture values nuance: the ability to differenzieren separates good scholars from mediocre ones.
hypothetical
Adjective. From Greek 'hypothesis.'\nKonjunktiv II is the grammar of hypothetische Situationen.

A: Is that a real or hypothetical situation?
B: Hypothetical. It did not actually happen.
A: What grammar do you need for that?
B: The Konjunktiv II.

The adjective form of Hypothese. Konjunktiv II past is the grammar of hypothetische Vergangenheit (hypothetical past). 'Hypothetisch gesehen' (hypothetically speaking) frames academic thought experiments.
interdisciplinary
Adjective. Inter- (between) + disziplinaer.\n'Interdisziplinaere Forschung.'

A: Is your research interdisciplinary?
B: Yes, it connects linguistics and computer science.
A: That is modern.
B: Interdisciplinary research is becoming ever more important.

Working across Fachbereiche. Modern academia values interdisziplinaer collaboration. 'Interdisziplinaere Zusammenarbeit' (interdisciplinary collaboration) is a funding buzzword. The best research often happens at the borders between disciplines.
'Hätte ... können' and 'wäre ... nachgewiesen worden' are hypothetical past. 'Stichprobe', 'Wirksamkeit', 'Methode' are nouns from verb or adjective stems.
To support a thesis with evidence. The verb that is the direct opposite of 'widerlegen' (to refute).
One must either substantiate or refute the hypothesis.
A compound noun: the first part names an academic discipline, the second means region or domain. An institutional division.
The first blank is the separated stem of a separable verb meaning 'to set up or establish'. The prefix lands at the end of the clause.
Count the grammar constructions: one uses a past hypothetical, one uses a noun form, and one turns a noun back into a verb.

Verumius closes the last book. He knows the terms. He understands the patterns. Had he not experienced the academic quarter on his first day, he would never have learned that knowledge in Germany has its own rhythm.
Verumius presents his research. Yilmaz reflects. Mia and Lukas chime in. Learn final vocabulary, contrast academic and informal register, and celebrate academic milestones.
