Celebrate academic milestones and review all constructions
Conference room in the department. Verumius stands at the lectern. His slides are clear. His outline is crisp. He has practiced this presentation fifteen times.
The research question of my term paper is: How does nominalization affect the comprehensibility of academic texts? On the basis of a sample of 200 texts I investigated whether...
A hand goes up. A question. Verumius pauses, thinks, answers. The professor nods. A colleague says: 'Good point.'
Verumius, that was excellent. Would you have thought two years ago that you would come this far?
No. Had someone told me that, I would have laughed. But here I am.
Later. Office kitchen. Colleagues congratulate Verumius. Mia texts in the group chat.
Lukas sends a voice message: 'The Roman aqueduct comparison? Typical Verumius. By the way, the music school is running. First students!'
the survey
Feminine noun. Um (around) + Frage (question).\n'Eine Umfrage durchführen' (to conduct a survey).

A: Did you conduct a survey?
B: Yes, 150 students participated.
A: What did you ask?
B: About their reading experience with academic texts.

You ask questions 'around' a group. Meinungsumfrage (opinion poll) is what news channels love before elections. Online-Umfragen have revolutionized academic research. Die Umfrage is the researcher's workhorse.
the variable
Feminine noun. From Latin 'variabilis.'\nAlways feminine in German.

A: Which variables did you investigate?
B: The dependent variable is readability.
A: And the independent one?
B: The degree of nominalization.

Statistical terminology borrowed directly from Latin. Always feminine in German. 'Abhängige und unabhängige Variablen' (dependent and independent variables). The foundation of experimental research design.
the database
Feminine noun. Daten (data) + Bank (bench/bank).\n'In der Datenbank suchen.'

A: Where did you find the texts?
B: In a linguistic database.
A: Which database?
B: The German Reference Corpus.

Where data sits on a 'bench' waiting to be accessed. 'In der Datenbank suchen' (to search the database). Modern research without Datenbanken is unthinkable. The metaphor: data resting on a bank, ready for withdrawal.
the deadline
Feminine noun. Borrowed directly from English.\nUsed alongside 'Frist' (deadline/time limit).

A: When is the deadline?
B: Next Monday, 11:59 PM.
A: That is tight.
B: Yes, but a deadline is a deadline.

Germans use it alongside 'Frist' (deadline/time limit). 'Die Deadline einhalten' (to meet the deadline). One of many English words adopted unchanged into German. The gender is feminine, following the pattern of 'die Frist.'
the lecturer (gender-neutral)
Masculine or feminine (der/die Dozierende).\nPresent participle of 'dozieren' used as noun.

A: Who is the lecturer of the seminar?
B: Dr. Mueller.
A: Is she a good lecturer?
B: Yes, very competent and fair.

The modern gender-inclusive alternative to Dozent/Dozentin. Participle used as noun: 'dozierend' (lecturing) becomes 'der/die Dozierende.' Part of the gender-neutral language movement in German academia.
the evidence, the proof, the receipt
Masculine noun. From 'belegen' (to prove).\n'Einen Beleg vorlegen' (to present evidence).

A: Do you have evidence for this thesis?
B: Yes, here is the proof: page 47.
A: Clear evidence.
B: Without proof, no argumentation.

In academic writing: a passage that supports your argument. In everyday life: a receipt (Kassenbeleg). The nominalized form of belegen. 'Belege sammeln' (to collect evidence) is what researchers and accountants both do.
the finding, the result
Masculine noun. Be- + Fund (find/discovery).\n'Der empirische Befund.'

A: What is the central finding of your study?
B: Nominalization makes comprehension harder.
A: An important finding.
B: Yes, and it is statistically significant.

More specific than Ergebnis: a Befund is what you actually found during investigation. Medical: 'Der aerztliche Befund' (medical findings). Academic: 'Der empirische Befund' (the empirical finding). What the data actually revealed.
the object, the subject (of study)
Masculine noun. Gegen (opposite) + Stand (standing).\n'Der Gegenstand der Untersuchung.'

A: What is the subject of your investigation?
B: The effect of nominalization on readability.
A: A clearly defined subject.
B: Yes, without a clear subject, no clear paper.

What stands before you for examination. 'Der Forschungsgegenstand' (the research subject) is more precise than 'Thema' (topic). The word implies physicality: something standing opposite you, waiting to be examined. Academic German at its most concrete-abstract.
to presuppose, to require
Separable verb. Voraus (ahead) + setzen (to set).\n'Grundkenntnisse voraussetzen.'

A: What does this course require?
B: Basic knowledge of statistics.
A: I do not have any. Is that a problem?
B: Yes, the course presupposes it.

What must be 'set ahead' before you can proceed. Separable: 'Der Kurs setzt Grundkenntnisse voraus.' 'Die Voraussetzung' (the prerequisite) is the nominalized form. German universities list Voraussetzungen for every course.
to compare, to juxtapose
Separable verb. Gegenüber (opposite) + stellen (to place).\n'Zwei Theorien gegenüberstellen.'

A: Do you juxtapose the two approaches?
B: Yes, I juxtapose them in the third chapter.
A: That is a good method.
B: Through the juxtaposition the differences become clear.

Placing two things opposite each other to see differences. Separable: 'Ich stelle die Theorien gegenüber.' 'Die Gegenüberstellung' (the juxtaposition) is the nominalized form. A powerful analytical tool in academic writing.
to acquire, to obtain
Strong verb: erwarb, erworben. Er- + werben.\n'Kenntnisse erwerben' (to acquire knowledge).

A: Where did you acquire these skills?
B: In my studies and through practice.
A: One acquires a lot through experience.
B: Yes, theory and practice together.

Strong verb: erwerben/erwarb/erworben. You 'recruit' knowledge to yourself. Formal register. 'Der Erwerb' (the acquisition) is the nominalized form. 'Spracherwerb' (language acquisition) is what Verumius has been doing this entire time.
the equality
Feminine noun. Gleich (equal) + -heit.\nNominalisierung: adjective to noun.

A: What does equality mean in the Basic Law?
B: All people are equal before the law.
A: Is equality the same as justice?
B: No, equality is formal, justice is substantive.

Article 3 of the German Grundgesetz guarantees Gleichheit vor dem Gesetz (equality before the law). Nominalisierung: adjective 'gleich' (equal) becomes noun 'Gleichheit.' Same pattern as frei > Freiheit, sicher > Sicherheit.
the justice
Feminine noun. Gerecht (just/fair) + -igkeit.\nThe -igkeit suffix on consonant-cluster adjectives.

A: What is social justice?
B: That all people have fair opportunities.
A: Is that realized in Germany?
B: Not completely. It remains a goal.

A core value in German political discourse. 'Soziale Gerechtigkeit' (social justice) is a central theme in every Bundestagswahl (federal election). The '-igkeit' suffix on adjectives ending in consonant clusters: gerecht > Gerechtigkeit.
the freedom
Feminine noun. Frei (free) + -heit.\nConstitutionally protected in Germany.

A: What does academic freedom mean?
B: Researchers may research and teach freely.
A: Is that a given?
B: No. Not in many countries.

'Akademische Freiheit' (academic freedom) is constitutionally protected in Germany, Article 5 Grundgesetz. The Berlin Wall's fall was a victory for Freiheit. Nominalisierung: adjective 'frei' becomes noun 'Freiheit.' One of the most powerful words in German.
the safety, the security, the certainty
Feminine noun. Sicher (safe/certain) + -heit.\n'Mit Sicherheit' (with certainty).

A: Can you say that with certainty?
B: Yes, with high certainty.
A: How sure are you?
B: The data give me certainty.

German values Sicherheit in all its meanings: social security (soziale Sicherheit), job security (Arbeitsplatzsicherheit), certainty of knowledge (Wissenssicherheit). 'Mit Sicherheit' means both 'with certainty' and 'safely.' A culture word.

Verumius presents: 'Anhand einer Stichprobe von 200 Texten habe ich untersucht, ob die Nominalisierung die Verständlichkeit beeinflusst.'

Lukas writes: 'Typisch Verumius.'

The same person, two worlds. Academic German: Nominalisierung, Konjunktiv, formal connectors (folglich, insbesondere, hinsichtlich). Informal German: short sentences, colloquial language, nicknames.

The skill is not choosing one style. The skill is mastering both.

At university you write 'Die Untersuchung der Daten zeigt signifikante Ergebnisse.' In the group chat you write 'Krasse Ergebnisse!' Both are correct German, but for different contexts. Academic German uses Nominalisierung, Passiv, and formal connectors. Informal German uses active verbs, short sentences, and everyday language.
Look for two different time references in the sentence: one looks back at what could have happened, one looks at what would be true now.
Apply the standard -ung nominalization to 'auswerten'. The result is the noun for systematically analyzing data.
The evaluation of the research results confirmed the hypothesis.
On the basis of these results we can conclude that... First a nominalization (basis/foundation), then a verb (to draw a conclusion).
